articles.title: Woman's Sphere
articles.author: Ramona W. Cannon
articles.complete_text:
DECEMBER! The month commemorating the birth of the Prince of Peace, finds the sad paradox of a world still at war! The statement of George Lansbury, Britain's pre-war pacifist, is worth pondering that whenever the will to peace becomes mightier than the will to war, we shall have peace.

This will is a qualitative, not quantitative, matter. Six years ago far more people desired peace than war; but the advocates of violence prevailed.

Women want peace. They now have more understanding of world conditions than ever before, and more influence. Their will to peace must be so intense that no effort will be too costly, no price, within the realm of honor, too great to pay for it.

SEVERAL women writers of Utah have won national recognition this year. Holt and Co. Have just published Peter's Story Goes to Press by Mrs. Olive Woolley Burt, a twenty-thousand-word story for juveniles. Her illustrated booklet-poem, God Gave Me Eyes, has sold more than 17,000 copies. Two of her forthcoming books are written about silver and wool.

Mrs. R. Easton Walker, poet, who writes under her maiden name of Courtney E. Cottam, received, in July, the Anne Hamilton Honorable Mention Award, from the Writer Magazine. In the next quarterly issue of the magazine, Poetic Out look, three of her poems will be featured with an article regarding her work.

AN interesting writers' enterprise is the Huckleberry Mountain Artists Colony located at Hendersonville, North Carolina, and conducted by Evelyn Haynes. Classes are offered for writers of short stories, articles, poems, art songs, plays or radio scripts. An annual prize is given in each field. This year, for the first time, the five judges were unanimous in awarding first place in the poetry contest to Mrs. Eva Willes Wangsgaard for her poem "Unforgetting Roots." This poem was one of 1,500 entries. She received a $10.00 award and spent a delightful week at the camp as a guest of honor.

Mrs. Burt, Courtney Cottam, and Mrs. Wangsgaard are all contributors to The Relief Society Magazine.

MISS Mildred Steelman of Salt Lake City won seventeenth place in the hundred prizes offered by the Writer Magazine for the best short-short stories. She was awarded $75.00 worth of books of her own choosing.

MRS. Dorothy Handley of Salt Lake City has published stories in the Saturday Evening Post, Family Circle, Romantic Stories, and in the Woman's Home Companion. One was reprinted in England.
articles.notes: This is a complete editorial column by Ramona W. Cannon discussing women's role in peace and highlighting achievements of Utah women writers.

articles.title: The Blessed Refrain
articles.author: M. C. S.
articles.complete_text:
THERE seems to be a spirit of love in the very air one breathes at Christmas time—a feeling of friendliness, a desire to help others, and a reaching out to those less fortunate. This spirit of true love pervades the air of the Christmas scene much as did the voices of the multitude of heavenly hosts nearly 2000 years ago who, praising God, said: "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men."

To many of the inhabitants of the earth, this year, this message may sound like hollow mockery. Even in this country there will be few families whose circle will not be smaller, lacking the dear presence of some members who may be in grave danger in far-off lands under strange skies, or who may have gone ahead to that eternal land toward which we are all traveling.

But to all saints who bear a love of Christ in their hearts and the knowledge that He is the Redeemer of the world, the first fruits of the resurrection, this heavenly enunciation should have added significance. Now especially should one render "Glory to God in the highest," and gratefully echo the refrain, "and on earth peace, good will toward men."

One must give "glory to God" as much as one in his humble way may—"For God so loved the world, that he gave his only begotten Son, that whosoever believeth in him should not perish but have everlasting life." As a result of this greatest gift, as a result of the great sacrifice, there may come to all the inhabitants of the earth that peace which the heavenly hosts proclaimed following the angel's announcement of Christ's birth; the peace of the soul which comes from a knowledge of God's eternal plan and a consciousness that one is living in accordance with His plan; the peace which pervades the soul within and is in nowise dependent upon conditions without; the peace to be found in bringing one's own desires and hopes into harmony with the will of the Father and acknowledging "the Lord knows best"; the peace that comes from a ready acceptance of conditions as they affect one from day to day and a willing submission to them; the peace that comes from showing forth love toward one's fellow man even to the extent shown by so many loved ones in this day who stand ready to lay down their lives for their friends; the peace to be found at all times and in all dispensations in the Gospel of Jesus Christ—"The peace of God which passeth all understanding."

With this peace in one's heart, of necessity one has good will toward men. Then let not the anxieties, the sorrows of this day obliterate the joy to be found in a true observance of this Christmas and an appreciation of the blessed refrain: "Glory to God in the highest, and on earth peace, good will toward men."
articles.notes: This is a complete Christmas editorial reflecting on peace and the Christmas message during wartime.

articles.title: Women and the Church Canning Program
articles.author: M. C. S.
articles.complete_text:
DURING the remarks made by President Clark at the opening session of the 114th semi-annual general conference, he estimated that fifty million cans of food had been preserved by members of the Church in 1943 for family and welfare use. He said: "For this tremendous achievement, the greatest that has come from a unified Church effort in a generation, the most credit and gratitude is due to the sisters of the Church who have done all the home canning as well as the bulk of the canning for the Welfare Program and the ward groups. We ask these sisters to receive the heartfelt thanks and congratulations of the whole body of the Church."

As the remarks of President Clark were heard by the women members of the Church who were privileged to listen to the conference broadcast, they must have recalled long hours spent in hard work at a welfare cannery or over a hot stove on a summer's day when they had heeded the advice of Church leaders and preserved food for the coming winter. A feeling of satisfaction must also have come to these listeners as they visualized the rows of fruit and vegetables preserved for their families' use.

Many of the groups who banded together to go to canneries consisted of members of ward and branch Relief Societies and many pressure cookers owned by Relief Societies were utilized by different ward groups. The General Board extends appreciation for the faithfulness and hard work performed by Relief Society members and the women of the Church in general who did so much to make this stupendous canning program a reality.

A mother could ask for no greater blessing than to know that her children will not hunger during the coming winter, and to those women who not only provided for their own needs but for others as well, additional blessings will be visited upon them. An editorial in the Deseret News, October 4, 1943 "Women of the Church, We Pay a Tribute" honored the women for the part they are playing today both in the Church and nation.
articles.notes: This is a complete editorial praising women's contributions to the Church's canning program during WWII.

articles.title: Across the Plains of Bethany
articles.author: Bertha A. Kleinman
articles.complete_text:
Across the plains of Bethany,
Across the waves of Galilee,
Hark, list, the angels sing!

Beyond the proud Jerusalem,
From Bethel to Capernaum,
List to the echoes ring!

Angelic hosts from out the sky,
Bring adoration from on high,
To hail the new-born King.

Peace, peace on earth, good will to men,
Peace, peace on earth, good will to men,
Hail, gloria, and sing!
articles.notes: This is a complete Christmas poem about the birth of Christ.

articles.title: Care of the Christmas Gift Plant
articles.author: Hazel D. Moyle
articles.complete_text:
CHRISTMAS will be made more joyous and happy for countless sweethearts, wives, and mothers by the gift of some lovely, living plant, blooming in cultivated perfection. Few gifts carry so eloquent a message of love and courage, or say so well that old, old greeting of "Merry, Merry Christmas," of "Peace on Earth." The Christmas plant has now become one of the most popular and treasured of gifts; never are flowers more appreciated than during this loved season when all our thoughts turn to home and loved ones.

"A thing of beauty is a joy forever" sings the poet, but alas! All too often our pleasure upon receiving such a plant is marred by the sad disappointment of seeing our lovely plant begin to droop and fade, often within a few hours after it is received. Why is it so difficult to keep these beautiful specimens of the professional florist's skill happy and contented in the changed environment of the modern living room? Why must the lovely, flaming poinsettia droop its great handsome leaves and vivid flower in such a discouraging manner? What makes the exquisite cyclamen, with its silken blooms poised like butterflies ready for flight, suddenly wither and become but a ruined shadow of its former glory; and why does that glamorous plant, the azalea, refuse to continue its thrilling show of fluted-petaled flowers and even discard its beautiful glossy foliage? These are the questions that have puzzled countless recipients of the Christmas gift plant.

We seldom stop to consider that the flowering plant has been living in the cool, moist air of the modern greenhouse, and that the change from this humid atmosphere to the hot, dry heat of the modern living room is a severe shock. Here are a few rules for the care of different gift plants. While no two are exactly alike in their requirements, one safe rule can be applied to all: namely—do not place your plant near a hot radiator nor in a too hot or heavily curtained room. Few greenhouse plants can endure the temperatures that we human beings find so comfortable. Drafts, too, are extremely detrimental, and many a well-meaning flower lover will stand a beautiful flowering plant in a draft-swept hallway, thinking that it is just the right place for the plant's well-being, when really it supplies the most harmful conditions.

This is especially true of the poinsettia, perhaps the most popular of all Christmas plants. Place your poinsettia in some warm, sunny, draft-free window, after first standing it in a pan of water until moisture shows on the surface of the soil. It should have a warm place which does not go below 70 degrees even at night. A lack of sun, chilling, and too much or too little water are the reasons why the flowers so often droop and fade. Water the plant thoroughly, using luke-warm water, then wait until it begins to dry out slightly on the surface before again applying a generous drink.

When the poinsettia begins to droop, after its flowers are all over, its resting time has come. Give it less and less water and remove it to some cool spot, the basement is ideal, where it can be given just enough occasional watering to keep the roots alive. When spring comes, cut your plant back to four inches and repot, using fresh garden loam, sand, and leaf mold or mountain soil of equal parts. Sink the pot outside in some spot where it can receive regular watering and soon it will begin to grow again. Be sure to bring the plant indoors before cold weather, and it will bloom again in time for the holiday season.

The azalea is becoming better known each year, and now rivals the poinsettia in popularity. This exquisite plant can now be purchased in sizes all the way from a small plant in a five-inch pot to the great specimen tree producing hundreds of lovely blooms. Should you be the fortunate recipient of one of these gorgeous flowering plants, do find a really cool spot for it. A sun parlor with indirect heat is ideal, but lacking this, place it in a cool window facing east or north. The azalea demands copious watering, twice each day not being too much. Some successful house-plant growers even keep the pot in a pail of water for a while each day. The roots of this plant are formed in a hard mat, and often the home gardener fails to give enough moisture to penetrate this hard mass. The foliage and stems of the plant should also be sprayed or sprinkled often.

Continue to give constant and plenty of moisture after the flowering period is over and remove the faded buds to prevent the seeds from maturing. If your plant begins to fade or look sick, give it a dose of the acid content which it needs—one-half teaspoonful of aluminum sulphate in a quart of water, and apply gradually. When good weather arrives, the azalea should be put outside, sinking the pot in a hole and filling up with peat moss which is sold by nurserymen and florists. Water the plant with one-half teaspoonful of any good commercial fertilizer in a quart of water every two or three weeks, and when fall comes, remove the plant to the house, and it will again put out its lovely crop of bloom. Some local house-plant fans keep azalea plants flowering year after year with such treatment.

The cyclamen is another favorite plant. This grows from a central corm or bulb, much like a gladiolus, and it, too, needs a great deal of moisture, but beware of allowing water to reach the central bulb from which the delicate fleshy stems grow. The best method of watering the cyclamen is to stand the pot in a shallow bucket of warm water for an hour, then drain the water off before placing the plant back on the saucer on which it stands. Never, never, allow water to stand in the saucer under the plant, and if the leaves or stems wither, jerk them sharply away from the bulb before they begin to rot, for this may spread to the bulb itself. Do not expect to keep the cyclamen happy in your living room, but place it in some really cool, light window, which faces east so as to avoid full sun. If you can keep the cyclamen cool enough, it will go on opening its abundant supply of buds for weeks, and may even develop new ones.

We are all familiar with the cheery little Jerusalem cherry plant with its gay Christmas colors of red and green, but sad to tell, this dainty plant usually presents problems to keep it from dropping all its leaves soon after it comes into our overheated homes, for this plant considers 50 degrees a comfortable temperature in which to display its bright berries. If you receive this plant for Christmas, place it at once in your coldest north window, and spray and sprinkle the leaves several times each day to supply the moisture which it craves. The pepper plant with its oblong berries that are found in all stages of growth and coloring, endures a warm room much better than does the Jerusalem cherry, but it, too, needs the same constant care of watering and spraying. A daily bath and plenty of moisture it must have in order to be happy.

One other plant is grown extensively in local nurseries for Christmas time, the improved begonia semperflorens. Grandmother grew this plant in her kitchen window, but how the modern plant wizards have glamoured it and made it sparkle! The plants we enjoy today are a dazzling mound of vivid color with the flowers almost obliterating the foliage. If you can find just the right conditions for this plant, it will go on blossoming for you all winter, and you can take cuttings from it for planting out in your garden in the spring where they will continue their amazing flowering week after week. Place the plant, during the summer, in your coolest window which has sun, but guard against allowing the hot midday sun to strike it through the glass. This plant, too, needs plenty of moisture, but should not be kept sopping wet, just constantly moist.

To keep these flowering plants in bloom is no easy task, as you can see, but it is well worth taking the time to give each plant somewhat the conditions it needs, for nothing gives a home such a gay and happy note as do a few blossoming flowers, especially when ice and snow are in command on the other side of the window pane.
articles.notes: This is a complete practical article about caring for Christmas gift plants, with detailed instructions for different plant types.

articles.title: One Star
articles.author: Della Adams Leitner
articles.complete_text:
The heavens were filled with myriad stars,
But one star led the way
Unerringly through unknown lands
To where the Christ Child lay.

And in this day of flickering lights
One steadfast beam from far
Leads Christward every soul who seeks,
God's love, His guiding star.
articles.notes: This is a complete Christmas poem about the Star of Bethlehem.

articles.title: Christmas Prayer
articles.author: Claire S. Boyer
articles.complete_text:
O God—man lifts his eyes tonight,
He lifts his mind in certainty
That Thou art the great ruling light
Encompassing humanity,
That Thou art love that warms the heart
Of every humble child on earth,
For as Thy symbol Thou did'st send
Thy word—through men—to earth!

O God, Thou gavest a perfect world,
But man, forsaking once Thy face,
Saw evil there and straightway hurled
Himself into a fearful place—
A world divided between good
And evil—hence his soul was torn
In twain, lost was his holihood,
So came his mortal morn.

O God, he could not see Thee, yet
He felt Thy presence ever near,
In labor he could not forget,
In suffering he seemed to hear
Thy voice, eternal, comforting,
Filtered through all his weary days,
And thus his self was given wings
And thus again he sought Thy ways.

Then in Thy blessed charity
In Thy compassion, Thou didst send
Prophets, with truth to make men free,
But few, alas, could comprehend;
So, as Thine own great sacrifice,
Thy Son was given up to man,
To plant Thy Kingdom, though the price
Be all—to give Thy plan.

Yet still man wavered in the right...
Thou wert so very far away,
Until man asked—then was new sight
Given, to hail a latter day!
For time had come for man to know
That in his soul was God's own shrine,
And "Ask and seek and knock" were so
Thou wert revealed! Divine!

O God, at Thy Son's holy hour
Of birth, again we turn to Thee
Thou hast revealed man's hidden power
That can transform humanity!
Our earth is wrapped in evil's shroud,
We see but darkly through the glass,
Yet will Thy light dissolve this cloud
And bring Thy Kingdom here to pass.

We, too, shall be the sons of God.
When unto Thee we bring the whole
Of love, when we transmute the sod
Of earthly thought to heavenly soul!
O God, tonight we kneel before
This dream—a Zion of Thy might!
Send forth Thy Word from shore to shore
And quicken all with this—Thy Light

"Ye are divine! Your strength must be
Godlike to carry out My plans!"
The world waits this reality—
Give it—tonight—to man!
articles.notes: This is a complete Christmas prayer poem reflecting on God's plan for humanity and the restoration of the gospel.

articles.title: Christmas Thoughts of Long Ago
articles.author: Emmeline B. Wells
articles.complete_text:
The following excerpts are from editorials by Emmeline B. Wells, published in the December 1890, and 1891 issues of the Woman's Exponent.

December 15, 1891—The people who dwell in these mountain vales have much to be grateful for even though they have some things not so agreeable. The children, for instance, their playhouses overflow on Christmas day; Santa has been very lavish in his regular supplies for the last few years, and our children of the present know very little of what the generation now grown up experienced in the early years in these valleys. Some of the many little stockings that were hung up around the primitive fireplaces were very scantily filled and others perhaps not at all. The mothers in those days didn't go out looking for Santa Claus, but many an earnest and heartfelt prayer was offered with tearful eyes and sorrowing heart, that the little ones might be happy and contented on Christmas day with what little it was possible to bestow.

December 15, 1890—We should be joyful at Christmas time for the sake of the children, if not for our own sake, and even if burdens oppress us or griefs have overtaken us, lay them aside for the time being and contribute in some measure to the enjoyment of others.... Children's impressions are deep-seated and strong and often follow them through life, and perhaps there is not a day that fastens itself more closely upon the heart and remembrance, and to which the child when grown to manhood turns with greater affection, than Christmas day at the old home, whether he goes out into the world to make his fortune or remains to share the family fireside.
articles.notes: This is a complete article featuring historical Christmas reflections from Emmeline B. Wells, comparing early pioneer Christmas experiences with later times.

articles.title: Theology - Articles of Faith Lesson 22: Religious Liberty and Toleration
articles.complete_text:
Tuesday, March 7, 1944

Article 11: We claim the privilege of worshiping Almighty God according to the dictates of our own conscience, and allow all men the same privilege, let them worship, how, where, or what they may.

Lesson Aim: To show that religious liberty and toleration are principles safeguarded by our inspired Constitution, and that they are supported by all Sacred Scriptures; that they enable men to inherit whatever kingdom of glory they desire in righteousness, but that intolerance is an enemy to God and impedes the progress of man.

CONDENSATION OF CHAPTER 22, TALMAGE'S, ARTICLES OF FAITH

Man's Right to Freedom in Worship
The Latter-day Saints proclaim their unqualified allegiance to the principles of religious liberty and toleration. Freedom to worship Almighty God as the conscience may dictate, they affirm to be one of the inherent and inalienable rights of humanity. The inspired framers of our charter of national independence proclaimed to the world, as a self-evident truth, that the common birthright of humanity gives to every man a claim to life, liberty, and the pursuit of happiness.

What is Worship?
The derivation of the term suggests an answer. It comes to us as the lineal descendant of a pair of Anglo-Saxon words, meaning worthy, and condition or state, and connotes the thought of worthy-ship. The worship of which one is capable depends upon his comprehension of the worthiness characterizing the object of his reverence. Man's capacity for worship is a measure of his comprehension of God. True worship cannot exist where there is no reverence or love for the object. Knowledge is essential to worship; man cannot adequately serve God in ignorance. Worship is the voluntary homage of the soul. Formalism in worship is acceptable only so far as it is accompanied by an intelligent devoutness. The most acceptable form of worship is that which rests on an unreserved compliance with the laws of God as the worshiper has learned their purport.

Religious Intolerance
The Latter-day Saints accept as inspired the constitutional provision by which religious liberty within our own nation is professedly guarded, that no law shall be made "respecting an establishment of religion, or prohibiting the free exercise thereof;" and they confidently believe that with the spread of enlightenment throughout the world a similar guaranty will be acquired by every nation. Intolerance has been the greatest hindrance to progress in every period of time; yet under the sable cloak of perverted zeal for religion, nations, while boasting of their civilization, and professed ministers of the Gospel of Christ, have stained the pages of the world's history with the record of such unholy deeds of persecution as to make the heavens weep. Christ, in His ministry among the people of Israel, saw with compassionate sorrow the spiritual and intellectual bondage of the times, and declared unto them the saving word: "The truth shall make you free" (John 8:32).

Intolerance is Unscriptural
The teachings of our Lord breathe the spirit of forbearance and love even to enemies. Hatred was not countenanced even toward foes. His instructions were: "Love your enemies, bless them that curse you, do good to them that hate you, and pray for them which despitefully use you, and persecute you; that ye may be the children of your Father which is in heaven...." (Matt. 5:44, 45)

Toleration is not Acceptance
It is in nowise inconsistent for Latter-day Saints to boldly proclaim the conviction that their Church is the accepted one, the only one entitled to the designation "Church of Jesus Christ," and the sole earthly repository of the eternal Priesthood in the present age, and yet to willingly accord kind treatment and a recognition of sincerity of purpose to every soul or sect honestly professing Christ.

Man is Accountable for His Acts
The unbounded liberality and tolerance with which the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints regards other religious denominations, and the teachings of the Church respecting the assurance of final redemption for all men except the few who have fallen so far as to have committed the unpardonable sin, thereby becoming sons of Perdition, may suggest the erroneous conclusion that we believe that all so redeemed shall be admitted to equal powers, privileges, and glories in the kingdom of heaven. Far from this, the Church proclaims the doctrine of many and varied degrees of glory, which the redeemed will inherit in accordance with their merits.

Graded Glories
To the apostles, the Messiah said: "In my Father's house are many mansions: if it were not so, I would have told you. I go to prepare a place for you. And if I go and prepare a place for you, I will come again, and receive you unto myself; that where I am, there ye may be also" (John 14:2-3).

A fuller knowledge of this subject has been imparted in the present dispensation. From a revelation given in 1832, we learn that three great kingdoms or degrees of glory are established, known as the Celestial, the Terrestrial, and the Telestial. Far below the last and least of these, is the state of eternal punishment prepared for the sons of Perdition (Doctrine and Covenants: Sec. 76).

The Kingdoms with Respect to One Another
The three kingdoms of widely differing glories are severally organized on a plan of gradation (See Doctrine and Covenants: Sec. 76; 131:1; also II Cor. 12:2). It is reasonable to believe, in the absence of direct revelation by which alone absolute knowledge of the matter could be acquired, that, in accordance with God's plan of eternal progression, advancement within each of the three specified kingdoms will be provided for; though as to possible progress from one kingdom to another the scriptures make no positive affirmation. Eternity is progressive; perfection is relative; the essential feature of God's living purpose is its associated power of eternal increase.

The Sons of Perdition
We learn of another class of souls whose sins are such as to place them beyond the present possibility of repentance and salvation. These are called sons of Perdition.

SUGGESTIONS FOR CLASS PROCEDURE

Suggested Songs
"Come, O Thou King of Kings," Parley P. Pratt, Relief Society Songs, page 1; Latter-day Saint Hymns, No. 158.
"The Lord Is My Shepherd," T. Kaschat, Arr., Relief Society Songs, page 29; Deseret Sunday School Songs, No. 212.
"My Prayer," P. P. Bliss, Relief Society Songs, page 35; Deseret Sunday School Songs, No. 294.
Solo—"In My Father's House Are Many Mansions," Mac Dermid.

It is suggested that for this lesson the class leader, prior to the class period, place on a blackboard in front of the room a brief outline of the lesson. At the beginning of the class period, the attention of class members should be called to this outline with the suggestion that they note the development of the lesson in accordance with the outline.

In the early editions of the Articles of Faith, by James E. Talmage, a series of questions and suggestive exercises were included. The 15 questions included for this lesson (Chap. 22) might be copied and at the beginning of the class distributed among 15 class members, one question to an individual. Persons receiving the questions should be informed that answers to the questions will be found in the presentation of the lesson; they should be told to follow the lesson carefully so as to be prepared to answer the questions when called upon to do so. Because of the great number of questions, answers must be brief. The class leader must then make sure that answers to these questions are to be found in her presentation of the lesson, and that her lesson be so planned as to allow time for answers.

Dr. Talmage's questions for chapter 22 follow:
1. What is worship?
2. Show that man's ability to worship rightly is a measure of his conception of God's attributes and powers.
3. Show that worship, to be valid, must be voluntary.
4. Demonstrate man's right to freedom in worship.
5. Explain the intolerance, in matters of worship, characterizing early and modern times.
6. Show that intolerance is unscriptural.
7. Demonstrate that tolerance is not necessarily acceptance.
8. Show that man, being free to choose for himself, is justly held accountable for his acts.
9. Explain Christ's expression, "In my Father's house are many mansions."
10. What kingdoms or degrees of glory are specified in the revealed word of God?
11. Who are to inherit the Celestial glory?
12. For whom is the Terrestrial glory provided?
13. Who will be consigned to the Telestial kingdom?
14. What is known of the gradation of glory within each of the kingdoms specified?
15. Who are the sons of Perdition? What is known of their fate?

Note: Class members should be urged to continue to bring to class copies of the standard works of the Church.
articles.notes: This is a complete Relief Society lesson on religious liberty and toleration, based on the 11th Article of Faith, with detailed theological content and teaching suggestions.

misc_summary: The remaining content consists of story continuations from other articles (including 'Thoughts on the Prophet Joseph Smith' and 'The 114th Semi-annual Conference'), a notice about Relief Society Songs being out of print, and what appears to be a truncated story continuation at the end. These are legitimate misc content as they are fragments or administrative notices rather than complete standalone articles.
